item.title: Astronomers of Imgur, I may have made a scientific discovery! I have found a predictive method to measure gravitational reflections which could help us understand gravitational lensing. Can you help this citizen scientist get attention to these images?
item.description:
This is the Coma Galaxy Cluster. I have outlined obvious gravitational lensing with colors matching to a corresponding repeated pattern. Gravitational lensing is the mysterious splitting and warping of light and imagery throughout spacetime, theorized to be caused by dark matter. This is known to cause several images of the same spacetime, however the deeper mechanics are still not clear as our understanding of gravitational physics is still incomplete.
Here is the unedited original for contrast. The Coma Cluster is home to plenty of repeat images caused by strong gravitational lensing. This is scientifically verified. Reflections created by gravitational lensing are typically visible in a line across the lens center of mass. This is the fundamental rule my measurement system is based on. Now I will introduce you to my method of measurement: the mirrored composite.
If one copies an image of outer spacetime and places it upon itself at half transparency, then flips the pixels in the layer in one axis, such as this image where the layer is flipped top-to-bottom, it should hypothetically naturally overlay any related reflections of one side of the gravitational lens upon its twin on the other side. Here's a demonstration with regular reflections on glass:
Both glasses have reflections on the surface, however the source of that light is the same, just warped by the underlying structure of the glass. Here's what it looks like with the composite process:
The process has effectively clumped the similar reflections together, and in so doing emphasized their relationship to each other. This is exactly what my composites of space are attempting to do; emphasize potential duplicate imagery that's not already identified. What evidence do I have that this actually works? Though the composites create artificial symmetry in one axis, the other axis is free from artificial duplication.. yet you still find alternate angles of the same structure, including identical star and galaxy patterns.
This composite is mirrored left-to-right, yet if you trace your finger down the mirrored fold in the center, you will notice going up and down that the structure is warped but identical. This works so consistently well that despite hundreds of composites, I have no examples yet where it fails to match along the fold, including at the scale of the Milky Way. Look at the coma cluster composite again, and this time look for the symmetry along the folded axis.
This image is overlaid top-over-bottom, so the white line indicates the axis where artificial duplicates live. Right now the white axis is unimportant. What you want to focus on is the axis indicated by red line running down the mirrored fold, where the overlay meets and mirrors. Along this red axis, right-to-left, is where the symmetry is natural and the relationships between reflections is clearest.
Due to universal nature of this effect, even while rotating the folded axis is somehow consistently quasi-symmetrical with the opposite end of the folded axis.
Nebula NGC 2818
"Dark matterless" galaxy
Numerous gravitational lenses.
Here's is one of our own Milky Way. The similarities from left to right are very apparent.
Time lapse of the center of our galaxy where a supermassive black hole lives.
Hoag's Object
Hamilton's Object
Hamilton's Object - Wide Field 1
Hamilton's Object - Wide Field 2
If you look carefully it's even visible within the CMB.
Orion Nebula
And one more annotated one of the Milky Way. Green lines point to similar positioned star, red lines highlight repeat structure. What do you think? Sound impossible? Try to debunk it yourself and show me your results! I'm happy to be wrong. In the end I just seek a scientist who might be able to answer this! Thanks for reading!
Bonus Image! Turns out if you take a picture of the Milky Way and then place a transparent layer, instead of reversing the image, just rotate it and the Milky Way turns into a helix. Who knew!
item.dc:creator: lensinggirl

item.title: When I came into the office and I was the only one there
item.description: I was fully prepared for it to be like last week, but luckily another colleague showed up and saved the day. But not before I could let out my inner John Travolta! And as lot of people asked for it: this dress is from Unique Vintage and unfortunately it doesn’t have pockets.

item.title: Too true…
item.description: So I live right next to the Galveston Bay, south east of Houston and this right here hits home. Recently Frontier has started construction in our city, to lay down and provide fiber for high speed Internet. Up until now, only Comcast XFinity was providing this service and they charged $134/mo for their service with no incentives, even for new customers. Now Frontier is offering $64/mo and a $200 gift card to switch to them and Comcast is losing their shit. I’m not so naïve to think that corporations will be corporations and it’s just a power grab for them in the long run, but I for one like the competition and can appreciate my bills not being so high. Just pisses me off that Comcast could have charged less for years but only now can “afford” to lower their prices. Welcome to America though, right? End of rant…
item.dc:creator: wisher1977
